title: The RP Gold Standard: What the MHRA Expects
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: The RP gold standard explained: what the MHRA truly expects of a Responsible Person on a UK WDA(H), from product knowledge to data integrity.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette set against deep navy, with a single subtle green accent light. A pristine temperature-monitored cold-chain storage area: rows of softly glowing refrigerated cabinets holding neatly arranged medicinal cartons, a sleek digital temperature display blurred in the foreground. Soft natural light falls from a high window, catching brushed-steel surfaces and a gloved hand reviewing a tablet device. Shallow depth of field draws focus to the foreground packaging while the aisle recedes into cool, calm shadow. Immaculate, authoritative and quietly premium. 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: What is the difference between an RP and an RPi?
faqs.a: The Responsible Person (RP) is named on a UK Wholesale Dealer's Authorisation and is accountable for general Good Distribution Practice compliance across receipt, storage and supply. The RP for import (RPi) is a distinct role required when medicines are introduced into Great Britain from an EEA country, and specifically assures that those products were certified by a Qualified Person in the exporting state or are subject to equivalent control. A single individual may hold both, but the scope of each should be documented separately.

faqs.q: Can a contract RP meet the MHRA's expectations for a site?
faqs.a: Yes. A contract RP can fully meet the gold standard provided the arrangement specifies a genuine time commitment, documented site presence and immediate contactability. The MHRA's concern is with absentee or nominal appointments, not with outsourcing itself. A clear quality agreement defining duties, authority and the right to halt distribution is essential to demonstrate the role is real.

faqs.q: How does data integrity apply to wholesale distribution?
faqs.a: The MHRA applies ALCOA+ principles to distribution records just as it does to manufacturing. Temperature logs, batch traceability and supplier verification records must be attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original and accurate, as well as complete and enduring. Uncontrolled spreadsheets, gaps in temperature monitoring and retrospective signatures are among the most common findings, and a credible RP treats these records as primary inspection evidence.
